document: Directive in respect of cybersecurity and cyber-resilisnce within the national payment system

sections.heading: Definitions
sections.summary: Defines critical service provider, compromise, cyber-attack, cyber-event, cyber-incident, cyber-resilience, cyber-risk, cybersecurity, cyber-threat, information asset, information system, information technology system, multi-factor authentication, payment institution, payment system Financial Market Infrastructure, operator, senior management, sensitive information, vulnerability assessment.

sections.heading: Background
sections.summary: Explains the role of SARB in payment systems, NPS structure, and evolving cyber risks in digitized payment environments.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.summary: SARB issues directive to impose cybersecurity and cyber-resilience requirements on payment institutions and systems.

sections.heading: Directive
sections.summary: Payment institutions and operators must develop and maintain cybersecurity and cyber-resilience frameworks with specific governance, critical asset identification, security measures, detection, response, recovery, testing, information sharing, situational awareness, and learning capabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires payment institutions to implement comprehensive cybersecurity frameworks including governance, critical asset identification, security controls, incident response, and regular testing to ensure resilience against cyber threats.

requirements.text: Payment institutions and operators must develop and maintain cybersecurity and cyber-resilience frameworks that include the following: cyber-governance, identification of critical operations and information assets, cybersecurity measures, detection, response and recovery, testing, information sharing, situational awareness, and learning and evolving.

Payment institutions and operators must have written effective cyber-governance arrangements that define cybersecurity objectives, outline people/processes/technology requirements, require board/senior management to determine cyber-risk tolerance, approve policies, oversee implementation, review annually, align with operational risk management, comply with standards, articulate cyber-risk controls, appoint senior executives, and ensure information sharing with SARB.

Payment institutions and operators must identify critical technology, operations, processes, information assets requiring protection against cyber-compromise, and internal/external dependencies to strengthen security and resilience.

Payment institutions and operators must ensure cybersecurity frameworks include security controls to protect confidentiality, integrity, and availability of services and information, with measures such as protective controls, access control, multi-factor authentication, encryption, and mitigation of interconnected risks.

Payment institutions and operators must ensure cyber-attack trigger points and detection measures to continuously detect and monitor anomalous events and activities, including multi-layered trigger indicators and detection controls.

Payment institutions or operators must have arrangements to enable safe and swift resumption of critical operations within two hours (or extended timelines with SARB approval) after cyber-attack detection.

Payment institutions or operators must develop and implement cyber-resilience testing programmes including test scenarios, penetration testing, system change testing, and regular vulnerability assessments.

Payment institutions or operators must share cyber threat and risk information with regulators, cybersecurity agencies, and trusted parties while ensuring compliance with data protection laws and the Cybercrimes Act.

Payment institutions or operators must understand the cyber-threat landscape, develop cyber-threat intelligence processes, and ensure collection of information about cyber-threats from other payment institutions to identify threats and develop detection, protection, and recovery measures.

Payment institutions or operators must ensure cybersecurity frameworks are adaptive, learn from previous incidents, monitor technological developments, and include predictive capabilities to proactively counter cyber-events.

Payment institutions or operators must report material cyber-incidents to SARB within 24 hours and provide a detailed report within 48 hours, including incident details, impact, recovery period, and systemic effects.
Payment institutions or operators must provide regular updates to SARB once incidents are remediated and operations have resumed.

sections.source_quote: Payment institutions and operators must develop and maintain cybersecurity and cyber-resilience frameworks that include the following: cyber-governance, identification of critical operations and information assets, cybersecurity measures, detection, response and recovery, testing, information sharing, situational awareness, and learning and evolving.

sections.heading: Supervision and compliance monitoring
sections.summary: SARB may conduct supervisory inspections on payment institutions or operators with prior written notification, access to premises, and authority to inspect documents and systems without consent in specific circumstances.
sections.dtia_summary: SARB has authority to conduct inspections with written notice, access to premises, and inspection of documents/systems without consent if certain conditions are met, ensuring compliance with cybersecurity directives.

requirements.text: SARB may conduct supervisory onsite or offsite inspections on payment institutions or operators to promote compliance with this directive.
SARB must provide written notification to payment institutions or operators prior to onsite inspection, specifying dates, SARB representatives, review period, and required documentation.
SARB representatives must produce a letter of authority and identity document upon entry to premises for verification.
SARB representatives may enter premises without prior consent for business operations or with prior consent for private residences where business is conducted.
SARB representatives may enter premises without prior notice if authorized by a warrant from a judge or magistrate.

SARB representatives may enter premises without prior notice if a senior SARB staff member believes a warrant will be issued, delay in obtaining the warrant is likely to defeat the purpose, and entry is necessary for the inspection.

SARB representatives have the right to access any part of premises, documents, and systems during inspections, including opening strongrooms, examining documents, questioning persons, and taking copies of relevant items.
SARB representatives must provide written and signed receipts for documents or items taken during inspections.
Payment institutions or operators may examine, copy, and make extracts from documents taken by SARB during normal office hours under supervision.
SARB representatives may require answers or documents from persons questioned during inspections, even if the person objects on grounds of incrimination.

sections.source_quote: The SARB may at any time conduct a supervisory onsite or offsite inspection on payment institutions or operators, in a form and manner that the SARB may determine, to promote compliance with this directive.

sections.heading: Effective date and non-compliance
sections.summary: Directive effective three months after publication; SARB may amend requirements; payment institutions must comply.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies effective date and compliance obligation for payment institutions under the directive.

requirements.text: This directive will be effective three months after its publication
Payment institutions or operators must comply with the requirements or conditions as stipulated in this directive
SARB reserves the right to amend any requirements in this directive

sections.source_quote:
Effective date and non-compliance
7.1. This directive will be effective three months after its publication. The SARB reserves the right to amend any requirements in this directive.
